event: Two shepherds discovering a skull with the inscription 'Et in Arcadia Ego'
symbols: skull on stone slab — death present even in paradise, the universal reality of human mortality
inscription 'Et in Arcadia Ego' — 'Even in Arcadia, I (Death) am present,' the classical memento mori warning
young shepherds — the living confronted by death's claim, innocence encountering mortality
dark landscape — the shadow that death casts over even the most idyllic existence
description: Two young men in pastoral clothing stand in a shadowy, wooded landscape, gazing down at a large human skull resting on a stone slab or tomb. The figure on the left, wearing a loose white shirt, leans against a tree with an expression of startled contemplation. The figure in the center, bare-chested with a red head covering and holding a staff, bends forward to examine the skull more closely. Faintly visible on the stone is an inscription. Behind them, the landscape opens to a dark evening sky with distant trees.
theological_description: While 'Et in Arcadia Ego' is a classical rather than biblical theme, its meditation on death's inescapability resonates deeply with biblical wisdom literature. Ecclesiastes declares 'there is a time to be born and a time to die' (3:2) and 'the living know that they will die' (9:5). The shepherds' encounter with the skull in a pastoral paradise mirrors the biblical insistence that no earthly garden escapes the curse of Genesis 3:19: 'dust you are, and to dust you shall return.' The vanitas tradition that flows from this image finds its theological root in the Preacher's cry, 'Vanity of vanities, all is vanity' (Ecclesiastes 1:2). Death as an uninvited guest in paradise also inverts the Eden narrative — sin introduced death into the garden, and no Arcadia can undo that fact apart from the resurrection hope.

scripture_refs.reason: 'Dust you are and to dust you shall return' — the foundational statement of human mortality
'Vanity of vanities' — the futility of earthly paradise without God's eternal perspective
'The living know that they will die' — the awareness the shepherds gain upon finding the skull
